edges.description: Abnormal shear and wall stress can promote endothelial and smooth-muscle dysfunction.
edges.source: Hemodynamic stress at cerebral arterial bifurcations
edges.target: Endothelial and vascular smooth muscle dysfunction

edges.description: Dysfunctional vascular wall cells contribute to abnormal matrix remodeling.
edges.source: Endothelial and vascular smooth muscle dysfunction

edges.description: Abnormal matrix remodeling weakens the cerebral arterial wall.
Progressive wall weakening can culminate in rupture with aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Rupture causes hemorrhage into the subarachnoid space.
Endothelial adhesion defects can weaken the cerebral arterial wall and contribute to hemorrhage risk.

edges.target: Acute hydrocephalus after aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage

nodes.description: Ruptured aneurysm management includes immediate pain and blood-pressure control, treatment of hydrocephalus when present, and rapid aneurysm repair by clipping or endovascular techniques.

evidence.reference_title: Current Management of Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: Treatment begins with immediate control of pain and blood pressure, placement of an external ventricular drain (EVD) in poor-grade patients and those with acute hydrocephalus on CT scanning, administration of antifibrinolytic tranexamic acid, and then repair of the aneurysm with either surgical clipping or endovascular techniques as soon as the appropriate treatment team can be assembled.

nodes.description: Ruptured aneurysm with subarachnoid hemorrhage can be complicated by acute hydrocephalus requiring external ventricular drainage.
nodes.id: Acute hydrocephalus after aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage

evidence.reference_title: Current Management of Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: Treatment begins with immediate control of pain and blood pressure, placement of an external ventricular drain (EVD) in poor-grade patients and those with acute hydrocephalus on CT scanning, administration of antifibrinolytic tranexamic acid, and then repair of the aneurysm with either surgical clipping or endovascular techniques as soon as the appropriate treatment team can be assembled.

nodes.description: Failure of the weakened saccular aneurysm wall releases blood into the subarachnoid space, producing aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage.

evidence.reference_title: THSD1 (Thrombospondin Type 1 Domain Containing Protein 1) Mutation in the Pathogenesis of Intracranial Aneurysm and Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: A ruptured intracranial aneurysm (IA) is the leading cause of a subarachnoid hemorrhage.

nodes.description: Maladaptive remodeling alters extracellular matrix organization in the cerebral arterial wall, reducing structural integrity of the vessel wall.

evidence.reference_title: Advancements in Brain Aneurysm Management: Integrating Neuroanatomy, Physiopathology, and Neurosurgical Techniques.
evidence.snippet: It provides a comprehensive overview of the pathophysiology of aneurysm formation and progression.

nodes.description: Rounded saccular outpouching of a cerebral blood vessel.
Cerebral arterial wall stress is associated with endothelial dysfunction and vascular smooth-muscle dysfunction, creating a cellular state that favors maladaptive remodeling.
nodes.id: Endothelial and vascular smooth muscle dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Advancements in Brain Aneurysm Management: Integrating Neuroanatomy, Physiopathology, and Neurosurgical Techniques.
evidence.snippet: It provides a comprehensive overview of the pathophysiology of aneurysm formation and progression.

nodes.description: Endovascular coiling or embolization is used to occlude selected aneurysms through an intravascular approach.

evidence.reference_title: Advancements in Brain Aneurysm Management: Integrating Neuroanatomy, Physiopathology, and Neurosurgical Techniques.
evidence.snippet: The discussion extends to modern neurosurgical approaches for treating brain aneurysms, such as microsurgical clipping, endovascular coiling, and flow diversion techniques.

nodes.description: Flow diverter devices are an endovascular treatment for selected unruptured intracranial aneurysms, with long-term studies reporting durable occlusion.

evidence.reference_title: Long-term outcomes of flow diversion for unruptured intracranial aneurysms: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Flow diverters are safe and effective in short- and long-term follow-up and rarely cause serious delayed side effects.

nodes.description: Abnormal hemodynamic loading is concentrated at cerebral arterial branch points, especially within the circle of Willis, where disturbed flow and wall shear stress initiate maladaptive responses in the vessel wall.
nodes.id: Hemodynamic stress at cerebral arterial bifurcations

evidence.reference_title: Advancements in Brain Aneurysm Management: Integrating Neuroanatomy, Physiopathology, and Neurosurgical Techniques.
evidence.snippet: It provides a comprehensive overview of the pathophysiology of aneurysm formation and progression.

nodes.description: Operative clipping excludes an aneurysm from circulation and remains a standard option for selected ruptured or unruptured intracranial aneurysms.

evidence.reference_title: Advancements in Brain Aneurysm Management: Integrating Neuroanatomy, Physiopathology, and Neurosurgical Techniques.
evidence.snippet: The discussion extends to modern neurosurgical approaches for treating brain aneurysms, such as microsurgical clipping, endovascular coiling, and flow diversion techniques.

nodes.description: Loss of vessel-wall structural integrity permits saccular aneurysm formation and enlargement, increasing susceptibility to wall failure.

evidence.reference_title: Advancements in Brain Aneurysm Management: Integrating Neuroanatomy, Physiopathology, and Neurosurgical Techniques.
evidence.snippet: It provides a comprehensive overview of the pathophysiology of aneurysm formation and progression.

nodes.description: Rupture of an intracranial berry aneurysm can cause hemorrhage into the subarachnoid space.

evidence.reference_title: THSD1 (Thrombospondin Type 1 Domain Containing Protein 1) Mutation in the Pathogenesis of Intracranial Aneurysm and Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: A ruptured intracranial aneurysm (IA) is the leading cause of a subarachnoid hemorrhage.

meta.association: Rare familial and sporadic susceptibility gene

evidence.reference_title: THSD1 (Thrombospondin Type 1 Domain Containing Protein 1) Mutation in the Pathogenesis of Intracranial Aneurysm and Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: TargetedTHSD1sequencing identified mutations in 8 of 507 unrelated IA probands, including 3 who had suffered subarachnoid hemorrhage (1.6% [95% confidence interval, 0.8%–3.1%]).

nodes.description: Rare THSD1 loss-of-function or damaging variants impair endothelial focal-adhesion and basement-membrane interactions, providing a genetically supported mechanism for vascular wall fragility in a subset of familial and sporadic intracranial aneurysm patients.

evidence.reference_title: THSD1 (Thrombospondin Type 1 Domain Containing Protein 1) Mutation in the Pathogenesis of Intracranial Aneurysm and Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: Mechanistically,THSD1loss impaired endothelial cell focal adhesion to the basement membrane.

evidence.reference_title: THSD1 (Thrombospondin Type 1 Domain Containing Protein 1) Mutation in the Pathogenesis of Intracranial Aneurysm and Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.
evidence.snippet: In zebrafish and mice, Thsd1 loss-of-function caused cerebral bleeding (which localized to the subarachnoid space in mice) and increased mortality.
